title: Was Classic Animation an indirect influence to Evangelion
display_title: Was Classic Animation an indirect influence to Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Was Classic Animation an indirect influence to Evangelion?             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Thu Nov 25, 2010 11:05 pm Okay, here's something that intrigues me about Evangelion; we all discuss on how Evangelion is a one-of-a-kind; something that is unique from other anime. But speaking as someone who grew up watching animation from America in the 30s to the 60s, I think what Evangleion did is nothing new; the idea of psychoanalyzing the characterizations of personalizes and the establishment of what animation was before something happen to change the ballgame is nothing new; Disney accomplished that by introducing techniques that we take for granted nowadays, Warner Brothers accomplished the same thing for taking those ideas and making it their own ideas (kinda sounds like what Ganiax embodies nowadays to anime) and even Hanna-Barbera accomplished that by establishing on how audience members viewed aniamtion for television. What do I mean by this? Well, it boils down to this; is it a possibility that we should thank Walt Disney, Bob Clampett, Chuck Jones and the like for giving the opportunity for a show like Evangelion to exist? I see the connections, and I'm willing to discuss on what these people did to animation that what Ganaix did to anime (for instance, psychoanalyzing characterizations; Jones and Clampett did that WAY before Anno was even thought of), but do any of you guys want to hear it? And for that matter, is my question valid?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Nov 26, 2010 2:18 am Ultimately, Japanese post-war animation as a whole was heavily influenced by the existing corpus of American work -- Betty Boop is the spiritual great-grandmother of the current generation of "moe-blobs". NGE purposely fed on the pre-existing state of the art, especially, but not limited to, the giant robot genre; so is no further than two steps removed.

[]: Re: Was Classic Animation an indirect influence to Evangelio             Quote Post by Xard » Fri Nov 26, 2010 10:01 am C.A.P. wrote: Okay, here's something that intrigues me about Evangelion; we all discuss on how Evangelion is a one-of-a-kind; something that is unique from other anime. But speaking as someone who grew up watching animation from America in the 30s to the 60s, I think what Evangleion did is nothing new; the idea of psychoanalyzing the characterizations of personalizes and the establishment of what animation was before something happen to change the ballgame is nothing new; Disney accomplished that by introducing techniques that we take for granted nowadays, Warner Brothers accomplished the same thing for taking those ideas and making it their own ideas (kinda sounds like what Ganiax embodies nowadays to anime) and even Hanna-Barbera accomplished that by establishing on how audience members viewed aniamtion for television. What do I mean by this? Well, it boils down to this; is it a possibility that we should thank Walt Disney, Bob Clampett, Chuck Jones and the like for giving the opportunity for a show like Evangelion to exist? I see the connections, and I'm willing to discuss on what these people did to animation that what Ganaix did to anime (for instance, psychoanalyzing characterizations; Jones and Clampett did that WAY before Anno was even thought of), but do any of you guys want to hear it? And for that matter, is my question valid?

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Nov 26, 2010 5:35 pm I also read (annoyingly, on a page that isn't accessible through a Google search because it's only available on the Internet Archive) that Anno enjoys Tex Avery, but either it wasn't cited or the citation only mentioned Tom & Jerry . However, I will say EoTV much more closely resembles 8½ than Duck Amuck (though I don't think anyone here's made a direct comparison to 8½ , the similarities between the endings are pretty comparable). The metafictional aspects most likely come from live action. But I don't think anyone (who knows much about film/animation history) credits Evangelion for being the first to put introspection and metafiction into animation anyway.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Dec 22, 2010 7:21 am NAveryW wrote: I will say EoTV much more closely resembles 8½ than Duck Amuck (though I don't think anyone here's made a direct comparison to 8½ , the similarities between the endings are pretty comparable). The metafictional aspects most likely come from live action.
